coredata.dc:title: Efficient synthesis of red-emissive nitrogen-doped carbon dots for sensitive detection of amaranth in food samples
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Inc.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Inc.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Qianchun
Liu, Jiaxin
Ao, Yongxia
Tian, Fengling
Tian, Xiaofang
Luo, Xinyu
Xie, Meijie
coredata.dc:description: Red-emissive carbon dots (CDs) play a crucial role in various food detection applications due to their strong fluorescence in the red luminescence region. Typically, nitrogen-doped CDs are synthesized from diverse precursors and predominantly emit blue fluorescence, which limits their advancement. In this study, we developed an efficient strategy to synthesize novel red-emissive nitrogen-doped carbon dots (R-NCDs) from L-malic acid and urea using a simple solvothermal approach. The synthesized R-NCDs exhibited optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of 540 nm and 630 nm, respectively, and achieved a quantum yield of 14.3 %. Additionally, the fluorescence of the R-NCDs was effectively quenched by the food coloring agent amaranth, allowing for a linear range of 0.050–75 µM and a detection limit of 15 nM. Furthermore, we established a new and efficient fluorescence detection platform capable of detecting trace amounts of amaranth in soda, fruit gummies, hard candies, vodka, preserved fruits, juices, and cocktails, yielding recovery rates ranging from 88.0 % to 119 % with a relative standard deviation of 1.1–3.9 %. Notably, this study is unique as it presents the first synthesis of R-NCDs based on L-malic acid and urea for the detection of amaranth content in various food samples, emphasizing its novelty and significance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Red emissive nitrogen-doped carbon dots
L-malic acid
Urea
Amaranth
Food samples

Determination of amaranth in real samples
Possible mechanism of R-NCDs examining Amaranth

XKJS202313, 23XYXS30, 23XYZD04).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The impact of various solvents on R-NCD synthesis, including water, ethylene glycol, triethylamine, and N,N-dimethylformamide.
(A) TEM image of a typical R-NCDs, the inset is an enlarged view of a particle. (B) the size distribution histogram of the R-NCDs. (C) AFM image, and (D) the corresponding height map for the R-NCDs.
Elemental analysis of the R-NCDs. (A) full XPS scan, (B) C 1 s, (C) N 1 s, with (D) O 1 s high-resolution XPS spectra.
Fluorescence properties of the R-NCDs. (A) Excitation–emission spectra. (B) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence graph. (C) Fluorescence stability. (D) Time-resolved fluorescence decay plot.

(A) Alone the R-NCDs about its fluorescence intensities, along with multifarious small molecules and ions, such as a) dextrose, b) citric acid, c) ascorbic acid, d) benzoic acid, e) Ca2+, f) Fe3+, g) NO2 -, h) saccharin sodium, i) glycine, j) L-cysteine, k) sodium cyclamate, l) sorbic acid, m) K+, n) Hg2+, o) sodium citrate tribasic hydrate, p) Na+, q) sodium polyphosphate, r) L-lysine, s) Cu2+, and t) amaranth; (B) The effects of possible interferences on the fluorescence intensity of the R-NCDs + amaranth, and the small molecules and ions, including a) dextrose, b) citric acid, c) ascorbic acid, d) benzoic acid, e) Ca2+, f) Fe3+, g) NO2 -, h) saccharin sodium, i) glycine, j) Na2EDTA, k) Zn2+, along with l) L-malic acid. (C) Fluorescence intensity about diverse concentrations of amaranth excited at various wavelengths. (D) Relationship between the amaranth concentration and F
0
/F, showing a linear relationship.

Linear plot between the concentration of amaranth compounds and peak area (A), chromatograms of Soda (B), Fruit gummies (C), Hard candies (D), Vodka (E), Preserved fruits (F), Juices (G), and Cocktails (H) samples. The standard at 0.50 μM (a), acquired with HPLC-UV (b).

(A) Fluorescence decay times of the R-NCDs alone and the R-NCDs + 75 μM amaranth system. (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of the R-NCDs alone, a 75 μM amaranth solution, and the R-NCDs + 75 μM amaranth system.
The preparation of R-NCDs for food samples application.
Determination of amaranth in food samples (n = 5).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: HPLC-UV was used to obtained the amaranth concentrations provided in parentheses.

head.ce:title: Efficient synthesis of red-emissive nitrogen-doped carbon dots for sensitive detection of amaranth in food samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Biology and Chemistry, Key Laboratory for Analytical Science of Food and Environment Pollution of Qian Xi Nan, Xingyi Normal University for Nationalities, Xingyi 562400, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Biology and Chemistry, Key Laboratory for Analytical Science of Food and Environment Pollution of Qian Xi Nan, Xingyi Normal University for Nationalities

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Biology and Chemistry, Key Laboratory for Analytical Science of Food and Environment Pollution of Qian Xi Nan, Xingyi Normal University for Nationalities, Xingyi, 562400, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Red-emissive carbon dots (CDs) play a crucial role in various food detection applications due to their strong fluorescence in the red luminescence region. Typically, nitrogen-doped CDs are synthesized from diverse precursors and predominantly emit blue fluorescence, which limits their advancement. In this study, we developed an efficient strategy to synthesize novel red-emissive nitrogen-doped carbon dots (R-NCDs) from L-malic acid and urea using a simple solvothermal approach. The synthesized R-NCDs exhibited optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of 540 nm and 630 nm, respectively, and achieved a quantum yield of 14.3 %. Additionally, the fluorescence of the R-NCDs was effectively quenched by the food coloring agent amaranth, allowing for a linear range of 0.050–75 µM and a detection limit of 15 nM. Furthermore, we established a new and efficient fluorescence detection platform capable of detecting trace amounts of amaranth in soda, fruit gummies, hard candies, vodka, preserved fruits, juices, and cocktails, yielding recovery rates ranging from 88.0 % to 119 % with a relative standard deviation of 1.1–3.9 %. Notably, this study is unique as it presents the first synthesis of R-NCDs based on L-malic acid and urea for the detection of amaranth content in various food samples, emphasizing its novelty and significance.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Amaranth, a common azo dye, has played a crucial role in food processing in recent years, owing to its good coloring ability, high chemical stability and low cost (Zeng et al., 2021). Amaranth is characterized by its unique, vivid, and eye-catching color, which render it important for coloring various products, such as drinks (Zhang et al., 2022), sodas, gummy candies (Constantino and Garcia-Rojas, 2023apple juices, and fresh cheeses (Soto et al., 2019). Although amaranth possesses distinct characteristics, significant appeal, and high nutritional value, its excessive consumption can cause serious health hazards, such as high genotoxicity, liver and kidney damage, and even cancer (Cao et al., 2020; Li et al., 2019). The World Health Organization and the Food and Agriculture Organization therefore recommend that the intake of amaranth should not exceed 0.5 mg/kg (Rovina et al., 2017). Therefore, there is an urgent need for a high selectivity and sensitivity, simple, and convenient method for amaranth detection and quantification in foods.

To date, several techniques have been developed to detect amaranth concentration, including capillary zone electrophoresis (Rovina et al., 2017), electrochemical analysis (Chen et al., 2023), thin-layer chromatography (Sigwela et al., 2021), liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (Ozgur et al., 2023), ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy, and fluorescence spectroscopy (Xiang et al., 2022; Yuan et al., 2023). However, the above methods have various limitations due to high instrument costs, lengthy sample processing time, and complex procedures. In comparison, the fluorescence probe method is preferred by some researchers because of its remarkable selectivity, quick detection, simplicity, and other benefits, securing it play a crucial role in multifarious fields. In this context, carbon dots (CDs) are important components whose qualities determine the selectivity, sensitivity, stability, and reliability characteristics of fluorescent probes.

CDs represent a novel class of carbon materials, initially discovered by Xu et al., which are composed of dozens to hundreds of amorphous and crystalline quasi-spherical nanomaterials (Xu et al., 2004). Due to CDs quantum size effect, which leads to a unique fluorescence, an outstanding solubility, and interesting electronic, physicochemical, and photoluminescence properties (Yang et al., 2024), these species can be employed in versatile application, including biological imaging (Mao et al., 2024), biomedicine (Li et al., 2024), tumor theranostics (Hussain et al., 2023), electrocatalysis (Bai et al., 2024), and food contamination detection (Kumar et al., 2024). However, CD precursors with high carbon contents are required for the synthesis of CDs with desirable quantum yields. Currently, a variety of CD precursors have been reported in studies, such as crayfish shell (He, Du, 2023), amino acids (Kumar, Rhim, 2024), L-malic acid (Zhao et al., 2024), citric acid, and L-cysteine (Zhang et al., 2023). Although heteroatom doping using nitrogen, sulfur, boron, and phosphorus, has shown promise for enhancing the fluorescence properties of CDs, the preparation of CDs with high fluorescence quantum yields remains a challenge (Gu et al., 2024). Furthermore, multifarious synthesis methods exist, including the laser pulse (Jin et al., 2024), arc discharge (Chao-Mujica et al., 2021), and electrochemical exfoliation techniques (Yu et al., 2024). However, the solvothermal method stands out for its simplicity, cost-effectiveness, and eco-friendliness. While CDs with blue excitation and emission wavelengths are commonly used for food detection, their application in the red luminescence region is limited. Interestingly, some studies have demonstrated the preparation of CDs from various precursors such as with excitation and emission wavelengths in this red fluorescence area. Red-emissive carbon dots show excellent biocompatibility for biological imaging and sensing, while red-emissive nitrogen-doped carbon dots (R-NCDs) exhibit enhanced fluorescence intensity and quantum yield. R-NCDs also exhibit lower photobleaching than blue ones, providing a more stable fluorescence signal (Wang et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2023; Zhong et al., 2023). This classification has sparked extensive research aimed at synthesizing efficient R-NCDs for detecting various compounds in food samples.

In the current study, we synthesized a novel and efficient type of R-NCDs using L-malic acid and urea as raw materials through a simple and environmentally friendly solvothermal method. Notably, the quantum yield of the synthesized R-NCDs was measured at 14.3 %. Leveraging the exceptional properties of R-NCDs, an efficient fluorescence method was developed for analysis of amaranth content in various food products, including soda, fruit gummies, hard candies, vodka, preserved fruits, juices, and cocktails (see Scheme 1). Additionally, a high-performance liquid chromatography equipped with an UV (HPLC-UV) results were in alignment with those obtained from the fluorescence tests. This discovery is a significant advancement in our understanding of the complexity and variability associated with amaranth detection in foodstuffs.

L-malic acid, rhodamine 6 G, benzoic acid, polyamide, sodium saccharin, L-lysine, triethylamine, and urea were acquired from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Calcium chloride (CaCl2), sodium nitrite (NaNO2), sodium citrate tribasic hydrate, sodium polyphosphate, citric acid, dextrose, zinc sulfate (ZnSO4), iron (III) sulfate (Fe2(SO4)3), sodium chloride (NaCl), ammonia solution (25 % w/v), disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetate (Na2EDTA), glacial acetic acid, N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), ethylene glycol, and copper (II) sulfate (CuSO4) were sourced from Fuchen Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). L-cysteine was provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Sodium cyclamate, sorbic acid, and amaranth were obtained from Yien Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Anhydrous ethanol was purchased from Xilong Scientific Co., Ltd. (Guangdong, China). Mercury (II) nitrate (Hg(NO3)2), sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3), ascorbic acid, potassium nitrate (KNO3), hydrochloric acid (HCl), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), and formic acid were supplied by Chuandong Chemical Co., Ltd. (Chongqing, China). Glycine was sourced from J&K Scientific (Hebei, China). HPLC-grade methanol and analytical-grade ammonium acetate were obtained from Dikma Technologies (Beijing, China). This study utilized ultra-pure water (18.2 MΩ cm), and all reagents were of analytical grade.

Fluorescence imaging was conducted using an RF-6000 fluorescence spectrophotometer from Shimadzu (Kyoto, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were captured with a JEOL JEM-2100 microscope (Tokyo, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using a Perkin Elmer-400 spectrometer (Shanghai, China). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were analyzed with a Shimadzu XRD-6100 instrument (Japan). The content of amaranth was further validated using an Agilent 1220 high-performance liquid chromatograph with an Agilent VWD detector (HPLC-UV; Agilent Scientific, USA). The fluorescence lifetimes of the R-NCDs were assessed with an FLS 1000 photoluminescence spectrometer from Edinburgh Instruments (Scotland, UK). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) measurements were performed using a JPK atomic force microscope (Bruker, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were carried out with a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB spectrometer (USA). UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained using a Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Japan).

The R-NCDs were synthesized using a facile solvothermal approach. Initially, L-malic acid (0.900 g) and urea (0.600 g) were dissolved in DMF (60 mL) and resulting the mixture was ultrasonically dispersed for approximately 10 min, transferred to a 100 mL polyphenylene-lined stainless-steel autoclave and placed in an oven at 180 °C for 8 h. After the reaction, the solution was cooled to room temperature (25 ± 10 °C). The resulting reddish-brown solution was subjected to centrifugation at 12,000 rpm probably 15 min. The reddish-brown R-NCD solution was collected for later use, then diluted to a concentration of 5.5 mg/mL and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C prior to use (Figure S1).

In order to elucidate the response of the R-NCDs to multifarious ions and small molecules, a solution (100 μL) containing 75 µM of dextrose, citric acid, ascorbic acid, benzoic acid, Ca2 +, Fe3+, NO2 -, saccharin sodium, glycine, L-cysteine, sodium cyclamate, sorbic acid, K+, Hg2+, sodium citrate tribasic hydrate, Na+, sodium polyphosphate, L-lysine, Cu2+, and amaranth, was mixed with an aliquot (1.90 mL) of the above-prepared R-NCD solution. Following this, the fluorescence spectrum of the resulting mixture was recorded under 540 nm excitation, and all measurements were repeated in triplicate.

The feasibility and practicality of using the synthetic red-emissive R-NCDs were evaluated by analyzing various food samples, including soda, fruit gummies, hard candies, vodka, preserved fruits, juices, and cocktails, all purchased from a local hypermarket. For solid samples weighing 1.00 g, such as hard candies, preserved fruits, and fruit gummies, extraction was performed using 15.0 mL of water. For the liquid samples, including soda, cocktails, vodka, and juices, 4.00 g was weighed and treated ultrasonically to remove CO2. Subsequently, citric acid (0.20 g/mL) was added to adjust the pH to 6, and the mixture was heated to 60 °C. Polyamide powder (1.00 g) was mixed with 1.00 mL of water to create a porridge, which was then added to the sample solution and stirred for 10 min. The mixture was filtered through a G3 sinter funnel. After washing with methanol/formic acid at 60 °C (pH = 4) five times, the solution was rinsed with water until neutral pH was achieved. The pigment was then desorbed using a mixed solution of ethanol, ammonia, and water until complete desorption occurred. The desorbed solution was subsequently collected, neutralized with acetic acid, and evaporated to near dryness before being dissolved in water to a final volume of 2.00 mL. All samples were filtered through a 0.22-µm membrane and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for subsequent experiments.

The HPLC-UV technology was employed to evaluate the concentration of amaranth in various samples, as previously described. Chromatographic separation was achieved using a Dikma Diamonsil C18 column (250 × 4.6 mm i.d.; 5 μm particle size; Beijing, China), with a mobile phase consisting of methanol and water, supplemented with 10 mmol/L ammonium acetate. The methanol proportion in the mobile phase was gradually increased from 30 % to 70 % (v/v) over a period of 0–8.00 min. The flow rate was maintained at 0.900 mL/min, the injection volume was precisely 15.00 μL, and the column temperature was set at 35 °C. Compounds were detected at an ultraviolet wavelength of 254 nm.

The study investigated the effects of various solvents on the synthesis of R-NCD through different solvothermal methods. A series of solutions containing 0.900 g of L-malic acid and 0.600 g of urea were prepared in 60 mL of water and organic solvents (DMF, ethylene glycol, and triethylamine) for the analysis of excitation and emission spectra. The results presented in Fig. 1 and Table S1 indicate distinct emission behaviors of R-NCD in water compared to those in organic solvents. Carbon dots synthesized in DMF exhibited the longest emission wavelength and the highest fluorescence intensity. This phenomenon is likely attributed to the high polarity of water, which impedes the formation of conjugated fluorescence centers, resulting in blue fluorescence for carbon dots produced in aqueous solutions. In contrast, the lower polarity of organic solvents enhances emissions at longer wavelengths. Moreover, nitrogen-containing solvents induce significant nitrogen doping on the surface of the carbon dots, creating an induced defect emission system that contributes to the redshift in emission wavelength. Additionally, DMF facilitates the carbonization and dehydration of precursors, leading to a more stable conjugated system. Therefore, DMF is utilized as the solvent for synthesizing carbon dots. Furthermore, an exploration of potential influencing factors was conducted to achieve optimal R-NCD performance, including precursor concentration, reaction temperature, and reaction time on the fluorescence spectrum (see Supporting Information Section 1 and Fig. S2).

The morphology of the prepared R-NCDs was examined using TEM and AFM. Fig. 2A clearly shows that the R-NCDs were well dispersed spherical particles without any noticeable agglomeration. These particles exhibited a distinct lattice structure, suggesting the presence of a well-defined nucleus. Subsequently, a more precise analysis of some single particles was conducted. The HRTEM image in Fig. 2A revealed that the R-NCDs exhibit a highly crystalline state with regular lattice fringe patterns of ∼0.21 nm, consistent across multiple experiments, which corresponds to the (1 0 0) lattice spacing of graphite-carbon (Boukhvalov et al., 2024). Additionally, the average size of these R-NCDs was found to be 3.7 nm, as illustrated in Fig. 2B. The AFM findings presented in Fig. 2C demonstrate that the R-NCDs are not only monodisperse but also lack significant aggregation, akin to quantum dots with single-layer functionalization. The average height of the R-NCDs in Fig. 2D was determined to be 3.6 nm, aligning well with the TEM analysis of average particle size. This analysis provides a solid foundation for conducting further characterization of the R-NCDs.

Subsequently, XRD and FT-IR were used to further investigate the surface states, as well as the elemental composition of the R-NCDs. As demonstrated in Fig. S3A, the XRD images distinctly show that the R-NCDs possess fractional graphitization, further proving that they are composed of not only amorphous carbon phases but also confirms the presence of graphite as indicated by the high-resolution TEM (Liu et al., 2019). Thus, based on our assumptions, the aforementioned statement was caused by the graphitization of carbon atoms and rich functional bonds on the R-NCDs surface. Furthermore, Fig. S3B shows the FT-IR spectra of the R-NCDs, wherein the broad peak at 3495 cm−1 was attributed to the O–H and N–H groups of the conjugated system (Jia et al., 2020). In addition, the absorption peak centered at 2935 cm−1 belongs to the stretching vibration peak of the C–H bond (Bouzas-Ramos et al., 2019), while the 1653 cm−1 absorption peak is ascribed to CO groups (Refat et al., 2021). Moreover, the peak at 1384 cm−1 corresponds to stretching vibration of the C–N bond (Shen et al., 2021; Ni et al., 2017). Additionally, the band centered under 1084 cm−1 is attributed to the vibration of C–O group (Wang et al., 2022). According to the analysis results, the surface of the R-NCDs features unsaturated carbon species and an abundance of polar oxygen-containing groups and nitrogen-containing groups, which render these R-NCDs water soluble.

The XPS was used to further elucidate the elemental composition of the R-NCDs. As explicitly shown in Fig. 3A, three major peaks appeared in the full scanning spectrum of XPS at 285.2, 400.0, and 531.9 eV, corresponding to C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, respectively, along with C, N, and O atomic ratios of 61.62 %, 13.27 %, and 25.11 %, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3B, the peak distribution diagram of C 1 s, the corresponding keys of the three peaks: 288.6, 285.8, and 284.7 eV, were attributed to O–CO, C–O/C–N, and C–C bonds (Kong et al., 2020; Mondal and Saha, 2019), respectively. Additionally, in the sub-peak N 1 s spectrum shown in Fig. 3C, peaks at 400.3 (Kong et al., 2020) and 398.7 eV (Wang et al., 2018; Zhu et al., 2013) correspond to the C–N–H and C–N groups, respectively. Moreover, it is represented in Fig. 3D that displays the high resolution of O 1 s spectrum, could be divided into three components about C–OH/C–O–C, C–O, and CO groups, associated with these corresponding keys are 532.5, 531.8, and 530.8 eV (Liu et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2021), respectively. These findings support the FT-IR results, suggesting that the R-NCDs are hydrophilic owing to the presence of numerous hydrophilic functional bonds on their surfaces, which are essential for their ability to detect ions and other small molecules.

Hereon, A
1 , A
2 , and A
3 contribute to the fractional components of the time-resolved decay lifetimes t
1 , t
2 , and t
3 , respectively. Consequently, this R-NCDs' average lifetime was calculated to be 8.92 ± 0.05 ns ( χ
2 < 1.2).

The R-NCDs' optical properties were investigated. For example, the fluorescence quantum yield was determined to be 14.3 % of that of rhodamine 6 G, as shown in Fig. S4A. This finding further supports the suitability of the R-NCDs for use in practical applications. Upon increasing the pH (see Fig. S4B) the fluorescence intensity of the R-NCDs underwent a notable change, sharply declining at pH 10 and beyond. This observation suggests the partial aggregation of the R-NCDs and a reduction in fluorescence due to reduced net surface charge. The optimal pH range for dispersion, and thus for ion detection and related experiments using the R-NCD solution was identified as 6.0–8.0. These results confirm the potential of the R-NCDs for use as nanoprobes, as highlighted by their promising optical stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Probe selectivity and detection of amaranth
ce:section.ce:para:
To investigate the selectivity of the R-NCDs, a 75 µM solution of an ion or small molecule (100 µL) commonly found in foodstuffs, including dextrose, citric acid, ascorbic acid, benzoic acid, Ca2+, Fe3+, NO2 -, saccharin sodium, glycine, L-cysteine, sodium cyclamate, sorbic acid, K+, Hg2+, sodium citrate tribasic hydrate, Na+, sodium polyphosphate, L-lysine, Cu2+, and amaranth, was added to 1.90 mL of R-NCD solution (5.5 mg/mL) for fluorescence intensity detection in Fig. 5A. The fluorescence spectra were captured under 540 nm excitation (n = 3). Additionally, a reagent blank was used to determine the fluorescence decrement (|ΔF|), which can be calculated as |ΔF| = |F
0 − F|; where F
0 represents the reagent blank (5.5 mg/mL of the R-NCD solution), simultaneously, F denotes the fluorescence of the ion-bound complex. It can be seen from these results presented in Fig. 5A that amaranth has an obvious quenching effect.

The feasibility of this method was confirmed by assessing its ability to counteract interference, as presented in Fig. 5B. Initially, a solution of metal ions and other small molecules (75 μM) was mixed with the R-NCD solution, which included dextrose, citric acid, ascorbic acid, benzoic acid, Ca2+, Fe3+, NO2 -, saccharin sodium, glycine, Na2EDTA, Zn2+, L-malic acid, and amaranth. Subsequently, a 75 μM solution of amaranth (100 μL) was added and mixed well with above small molecules and ions system, respectively. The fluorescence intensities of the system mixed with the potential interferences mentioned above were measured under 540-nm excitation. Interestingly, the results revealed that the presence of interfering substances at the same concentration as the amaranth did not affect the quenching effect of amaranth. Notably, the R-NCDs exhibited excellent anti-interference ability for amaranth detection. Furthermore, as the concentration of amaranth increased from 0 to 75 µM, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased in Fig. 5C as shown, and the quenching relationship could be represented by the linear correlation given in Eq. (2):
(2)
Y = F
0
/F = 0.03415 C + 1.03064

Herein, F
0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of the R-NCDs under 540-nm excitation without amaranth and in the presence of amaranth, respectively, while C denotes the concentration of amaranth. As illustrated in Fig. 5D, the calibration curve perfectly revealed a perfect linear range of 0.050–75 µM for amaranth measurement, accompanied by a regression coefficient of 0.998, and the limit of detection was found to be 15 nM based on 3S/N. In addition, this proposed method was compared with previous methods for amaranth measurement (Xiang et al., 2022; Yuan et al., 2023; Alves et al., 2008; Tian et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2010; Zhang et al., 2014). As shown in Table S2, the probe method demonstrated excellent performance, including high sensitivity and a remarkable correlation coefficient. Consequently, the above results demonstrate the potential of these synthetic R-NCDs for use as sample analysis probes in various fields.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of amaranth in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Subsequently, a detection platform based on the R-NCDs was developed to assess the amaranth contents of multifarious common food samples, including soda, fruit gummies, hard candies, vodka, preserved fruits, juices, and cocktails. The exceptional sensitivity of the R-NCDs to amaranth enabled the fluorescence detection at an emission wavelength of 630 nm (excitation at 540 nm). Refer to Table 1 for specifics; concentrations in soda, fruit gummies, hard candies, vodka, preserved fruits, juices, and cocktails were 1.13, 0.830, 1.53, 0.115, 0.199, 0.0251, and 0.026 μg/g, respectively. With recovery rates ranging from 88.0 % to 119 %, in addition to an RSD < 3.9 % (n = 5), these results clearly highlight the effectiveness of this fluorescence method in terms of accuracy and feasibility. Simultaneously, HPLC-UV was also applied to determine the amaranth concentrations in seven common foodstuffs. Fig. 6A demonstrates a linear range of 0.050–75 μM and an unrivaled linearity (R 2 = 0.9997), thereby indicating that this technique is suitable for analyzing the amaranth content in complex samples (see Fig. 6B–6 H). The HPLC-UV results were closely aligned with those obtained using the synthetic R-NCDs approach, indicating that the R-NCDs can be employed for reliable quantitative colorant analysis of foodstuffs, and expanding its potential applications in the area of food safety.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of R-NCDs examining Amaranth
ce:section.ce:para: The exploration of the quenching mechanism between the R-NCDs and amaranth necessitates thorough research and analysis. As illustrated in Fig. 7 A, the fluorescence lifetimes for the R-NCDs and the R-NCDs combined with 75 μM amaranth were 8.92 and 8.50 ns, respectively, with a difference of 0.42 ns. Interestingly, this phenomenon indicates that the occurrence of an internal filtering effect, which could be interpreted as a potential quenching mechanism (Korah and Mathew, 2023). Simultaneously, Comparison of the UV–vis spectrograms recorded for the R-NCDs alone and the R-NCDs + 75 μM amaranth system revealed a significant overlap in the absorption bands, suggesting that amaranth acts as a rapid quenching agent. Furthermore, the detailed examination in Fig. 7B revealed the presence of a new peak at 513 nm in the UV–vis absorption for the R-NCDs + 75 μM amaranth system, which could be attributed to an interaction between amaranth and the R-NCDs ultimately resulting in fluorescence quenching.

In conclusion, R-NCDs were successfully synthesized using L-malic acid and urea through a solvothermal approach, demonstrating remarkable photoluminescence and a high quantum yield. The selectivity and sensitivity of the R-NCDs towards amaranth facilitated the development of an effective fluorescence detection platform for monitoring amaranth in various food products, including soda, fruit gummies, hard candies, vodka, preserved fruits, juices, and cocktails. Furthermore, HPLC-UV was employed to validate the results obtained from the fluorescence method, corroborating the efficacy of the detection platform. These promising results underscore the excellent potential of R-NCDs for accurate detection of amaranth content in foodstuffs, paving the way for future applications in food safety and quality control.

Meijie Xie: Formal analysis. Xiaofang Tian: Formal analysis. Xinyu Luo: Formal analysis. Yongxia Ao: Formal analysis, Data curation. Fengling Tian: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Qianchun Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Jiaxin Liu: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors have no conflicting interests or relationships influencing the work.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to express their gratitude for the financial support received from the Science and Technology Foundation of the Guizhou Provincial Basic Research Program(Natural Science)ZK[2024] (No.666), the University Science and Technology Innovation Team Project of the Guizhou Provincial Education Department (Qianjiaoji[2023]095), the Science and Technology Bureau of Qian Xi Nan Prefecture (Nos. 2021-2-31 and 2023-2-4), and the Projects of Xingyi Normal University for Nationalities (Nos. XKJS202313, 23XYXS30, 23XYZD04).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jfca.2024.107113.
